title: OLAP versus OLTP workloads
description: Why analytical (columnar, scan-heavy) and transactional (row, point-write) systems are tuned oppositely.
body: Use when deciding where a workload belongs. OLTP systems handle many small point reads and writes on row-oriented storage with B-tree indexes; OLAP warehouses run few large aggregating scans on columnar storage optimized for bytes scanned. Do not run heavy analytical scans against the production OLTP database — it competes with transactions and lacks columnar speed. Actions: route aggregations to the warehouse, keep transactions on the OLTP store, and replicate via ELT. Validation signals include analytical queries hitting the warehouse and the OLTP store free of scan-heavy reporting load. Risk boundary: analytics on the OLTP primary can starve live transactions; if reporting hits production, escalate to offload it before it degrades the application.
